html:
Lu Thai Textile B-Share Thesis

Lu Thai Textile’s B-share is a mispriced equal economic claim on a conservatively financed Chinese premium fabric and garment manufacturer, not the HKD12.50 stock described in the stale prior thesis. The current reference price is about HKD4.48 (Investing.com quote), which is only about RMB3.88 per share. Against 2025 attributable book value of RMB9.81 billion, or roughly RMB12.0 per share, the B-share trades near 0.32x book.

The business is not exciting on the surface. 2025 revenue fell 3.1% to RMB5.90 billion, and 2025 core net profit fell 19.5% to RMB388 million, while reported net profit rose 44.7% to RMB594 million because of financial-asset gains (2025 annual report). Q1 2026 was not a clean inflection either: revenue declined 6.7%, net profit fell 36.9% to RMB93.7 million, and core profit fell 20.4% to RMB70.6 million (Q1 summary).

That weakness is exactly why the setup is interesting. The market is pricing Lu Thai as a dying apparel exporter, but the company is still generating cash, still has hard asset coverage, and is already repositioned for the tariff and labor-cost world that investors fear. Operating cash flow was RMB712 million in 2025 and RMB162 million in Q1 2026, up 51% year over year. The company has moved 32% of fabric capacity overseas, mainly Vietnam, and 72% of garment capacity overseas across Vietnam, Cambodia, and Myanmar, while a 3 million-piece overseas garment line entered trial production in 2024 and a Vietnam high-end fabric project has been built out. This is not a pure China labor-cost bet anymore. It is a vertical textile platform with overseas assembly, premium fabric know-how, and underutilized capacity.

The variant perception is that Lu Thai does not need a growth-stock outcome. It only needs to stop being valued like impaired inventory. At HKD4.48, the B-share is at roughly 5.3x 2025 reported EPS, roughly 5.9x TTM EPS, and around 8-9x depressed core earnings, while paying a proposed RMB0.15 per share 2025 dividend, equal to about HKD0.17 before tax translation. The dividend yield is not the whole thesis, but it pays investors to wait while book value compounds.

The post-Q1 balance sheet needs to be handled carefully. The company had large cash and trading financial assets, but the convertible bond redemption and delisting will consume cash. Management said on the April 30 investor call that the completed convertible repayment should reduce interest expense in later quarters, while Q1 financial expense was hurt by RMB appreciation and FX losses (investor record). So this is not a lazy “negative EV” thesis. The better argument is that the B-share price already capitalizes the operating business at a distressed multiple while balance-sheet assets and book value sharply limit permanent impairment.

There is also a shareholder-return angle. Lu Thai cancelled 23.9 million B-shares in 2023 and 46.2 million B-shares in 2024, highly accretive repurchases at deep discounts to book. 2025 did not bring another major B-share buyback, which is a legitimate frustration, but Chinese retail/news flow shows investors are still pushing management on B-share repurchases. A renewed buyback at HKD4-5 would be far more value-creating than sitting on excess liquidity.

The upside path is straightforward. Fabric utilization was only 73% in 2025, versus garment utilization of 92%. If global apparel demand merely stabilizes, Vietnam and garment mix continue ramping, the functional fabric/new-materials project keeps narrowing losses, and FX/convertible interest drag normalizes, core earnings can move from a depressed RMB370-400 million run-rate toward RMB550-650 million without heroic revenue growth. On the current B-share price, that is a forward core P/E around 5-6x. A modest rerating to 0.55-0.65x book or 9-10x normalized earnings gets the stock close to a double.

The bull case is not fantasy. The B-share structure itself is the embedded option. B-shares are illiquid, foreign-currency-denominated, and ignored, which is why this share class can trade at a massive discount to the underlying asset value. Any renewed B-share buyback, B-share reform discussion, privatization proposal, or sustained dividend upgrade could force investors to value the same economic claim closer to A-share or book-value reality.

The risks are real. A stronger RMB hurts translated export profits. A renewed demand slowdown would keep fabric utilization weak. Tariffs could shift from finished garments into upstream fabric. Management could keep too much cash idle or allocate it to low-return financial assets instead of buybacks and dividends. The B-share can also stay cheap for longer than fundamentals justify. But those are timing and capital-allocation risks more than solvency risks.

At HKD4.48, Lu Thai B is a small, ignored, cash-rich, asset-backed tariff-repositioning story trading at a fraction of book. The stock is not priced for a textile recovery, not priced for buybacks, not priced for A/B convergence, and not priced for even average capital discipline.

Targets

Bear: HKD3.20, -29%. Core profit falls toward RMB250-300 million, no buyback returns, RMB strength persists, and the B-share remains stuck near 0.25x book.

Base: HKD8.50, +90%. Core profit recovers toward RMB550-600 million, post-convertible interest drag fades, Vietnam capacity proves useful, and the B-share rerates to roughly 0.6x book.

Bull: HKD14.00, +213%. Earnings power reaches RMB700-800 million, management resumes aggressive B-share repurchases or a structural B-share solution emerges, and the stock rerates toward book value.
